title: Colony Ship: A Post-Earth Role Playing Game

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Colony Ship as an 'isometric, party-based RPG' with turn-based play, a detailed skill-based character system, multiple ways to handle quests, branching dialogue trees, and choices-and-consequences faction negotiation aboard a generation ship. That combination strongly fits a party-based CRPG: isometric perspective, party control, authored branching quest structure, tactical/methodical turn-based encounters, and heavy dialogue-choice driven progression. Secondary overlap with western_narrative_rpg and tactical_rpg is present, but the party-based isometric CRPG identity is the clearest core gameplay profile.

overall_note: The set mostly understands Colony Ship as a modern old-school CRPG: isometric, tactical, choice-driven, and build-sensitive. The strongest picks emphasize reactive quests and punishing turn-based play; the weaker end drifts toward either more linear mission-based structure or more solitary exploration/combat emphasis than Colony Ship’s party-and-reputation driven CRPG loop.
anchor_summary: Colony Ship: A Post-Earth Role Playing Game is a hardcore isometric CRPG centered on turn-based tactical combat, skill-check-heavy quest resolution, consequential dialogue choices, faction politics, and party-based progression in a single-player campaign.
gap_notes: The rail has good hardcore CRPG coverage but leans unevenly between party-based reactive RPGs and more solitary exploration-heavy RPGs.
The weakest matches miss some of Colony Ship’s specific identity as a dialogue-heavy, faction-reactive, party-based CRPG rather than a pure combat/exploration game.
A tighter rail would prioritize games with both strong quest reactivity and comparable party/tactical structure.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Near-direct sibling in design DNA: hardcore turn-based CRPG, dense skill-based builds, many quest solutions, heavy choices-and-consequences, and similar overall challenge model.

Very solid party-based isometric RPG comp with turn-based tactical combat, squad building, dialogue checks, and major faction/quest reactivity, though it is broader and more combat-forward than Colony Ship.

Classic-style sci-fi isometric CRPG with build-driven progression, branching quest resolution, factions, and tactical turn-based encounters; a strong thematic and structural neighbor despite being somewhat looser in polish and party identity.
Shares isometric squad-based turn-based combat and story choices, but its hub-and-mission cadence and more directed campaign make it less like Colony Ship’s broader reactive CRPG questing.

A credible hardcore isometric turn-based RPG comparison for build depth, difficulty, and hostile-world progression, but it is more solitary and exploration/combat-driven than Colony Ship’s party-based conversational CRPG structure.

title: Terra Invicta

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Terra Invicta as a long-form strategic campaign centered on faction-level control of Earth's nations, covert political influence, global research direction, proxy conflicts, Solar System expansion, and base/station construction. The text repeatedly emphasizes geopolitics as a sandbox, detailed regional simulation, councilor management, control points, colonization of asteroids/moons/planets, and tactical fleet combat. That macro-scale political and imperial management profile fits grand_strategy best, with strong 4X overlap from expansion, resource extraction, research, and space colonization. Evidence is rich and concrete across Steam, OpenCritic, and Metacritic-derived stored text, not merely genre tags.

overall_note: The set is directionally solid for a complex space-strategy anchor, with several credible grand-strategy and 4X neighbors. The biggest gap is that most candidates match the space empire-management side better than Terra Invicta’s distinctive Earth-first geopolitical infiltration and councilor-driven shadow politics.
anchor_summary: Terra Invicta is a dense single-player grand-strategy/4X hybrid built around covert faction control of Earth, geopolitical influence, long-horizon research and infrastructure planning, Solar System expansion, and tactical fleet warfare in a pausable macro campaign.
gap_notes: No candidate closely mirrors Terra Invicta's blend of clandestine political control on modern Earth plus later Solar System expansion.
Most candidates are more conventional empire 4X games, starting as overt state/civilization management rather than covert faction influence.
The rail is strongest on macro strategy, research, diplomacy, and fleet management, but weaker on matching Terra Invicta's hybrid structure and tactical-combat context.

Very strong fit on dense pausable real-time 4X systems, economy, ship design, colonization, and long-form strategic management; weaker on political manipulation and Earth-centric progression.

title: ARMA 3 Contact

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify this as a spin-off expansion built in 'authentic Arma 3 style,' centered on a military science-fiction campaign with combat recon, electronic warfare, new factions, weapons, vehicles, and a large sandbox terrain suited for combined arms warfare. Store labels also confirm single-player, co-op, and PvP support. The strongest gameplay signals are tactical realism, combat-focused military operations, open sandbox terrain, and methodical shooter play, which fit tactical_shooter best; military_fps is a close secondary fit.

overall_note: The set is directionally solid for realistic tactical military shooters, but it misses the most obvious must-have comparison: the actual base game Arma 3. The provided candidates are all understandable neighbors, yet the top result is another Arma 3 expansion rather than the parent game itself.
anchor_summary: ARMA 3 Contact is a standalone expansion/DLC-like entry built on Arma 3’s milsim identity: first-person tactical military sandbox, realistic combined-arms combat, large terrains, vehicles, recon, and simulation-heavy mission play, with a more authored sci-fi investigation campaign layered on top.
gap_notes: The rail appears to be missing the actual base game Arma 3, which is the clearest must-include for Contact.
Arma 3 Apex is extremely close in mechanics and platform identity, but it is not the base game; treating it as such would be inaccurate.
Squad matches realism, teamwork, large maps, and combined arms, but its identity is much more PvP/session-driven than Contact’s sandbox-plus-authored-campaign structure.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Another Arma 3 expansion sharing the same underlying milsim gunplay, vehicles, large-map sandbox structure, and tactical mission design. Extremely close mechanically, though not the actual base game.

title: RimWorld - Royalty

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: This is additive DLC for RimWorld and does not contradict the parent game's core identity, so the parent gameplay should drive classification. Parent text explicitly describes RimWorld as a 'sci-fi colony sim' where you manage colonists' needs and relationships, build and craft a settlement, trade, tame animals, form caravans, and respond to raids and other systemic threats. Royalty adds quests, psychic powers, noble titles, throne rooms, hospitality scenarios, and more combat/event variety, but these extend the same colony-management and story-generation loop rather than establishing a different genre. The combined evidence strongly supports colony_sim with systemic sandbox management, emergent narrative, settlement building, colony growth, and tactical encounter handling.

overall_note: The set contains the essential base game comp and several genuinely strong colony-sim neighbors. Ordering should prioritize the direct parent first, then the closest emergent-colony management sandboxes, with broader management sims lower.
anchor_summary: RimWorld - Royalty is direct DLC for RimWorld, so the anchor's gameplay identity remains a colony sim: top-down pawn management, emergent story generation, base building, survival pressures, production chains, and tactical raid defense, with Royalty adding quests, noble systems, and psychic combat options on top.
gap_notes: RimWorld itself is a mandatory inclusion because Royalty is additive DLC rather than a standalone gameplay identity.
Dwarf Fortress, Stranded: Alien Dawn, and Going Medieval all fit the colony-management/survival sandbox profile well, though each is a bit looser than the RimWorld base game.
Prison Architect is understandable through top-down management and emergent incidents, but it lacks RimWorld's survival-colony loop, expedition structure, and combat emphasis.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Direct parent game of the DLC and effectively the same core colony-sim loop, systems, perspective, progression, and audience.

title: Total War: THREE KINGDOMS - Eight Princes

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Accepted as grand_strategy using DLC inheritance from the parent game Total War: THREE KINGDOMS, since Eight Princes is clearly an additive chapter pack rather than a genre-shifting mode. The DLC text repeatedly describes new campaign mechanics, princes, buildings, assignments, court options, faction advancement, alignments, and elite units, which matches the parent game's core loop. Parent evidence is strong and explicit: THREE KINGDOMS combines a turn-based campaign of empire-building, statecraft, and conquest with real-time battles. That supports grand_strategy as the primary identity, with rts as a secondary flavor for battlefield engagements. Historical setting, long-form campaign structure, systemic faction management, settlement development, and tactical optimization are all directly supported by the stored descriptions.

overall_note: The rail is very strong: it includes the required base game and then stays within closely related Total War titles that share the same campaign-map plus real-time battle identity. Ordering mostly reflects gameplay proximity, with historical Total War entries fitting slightly better than the fantasy branch.
anchor_summary: Total War: THREE KINGDOMS - Eight Princes is a DLC campaign for a hybrid strategy game built around turn-based grand-campaign management and diplomacy plus large-scale real-time battles, with faction progression, character systems, and long-form single-player conquest as the core loop.
gap_notes: Base game inclusion is essential here because Eight Princes is additive DLC rather than a standalone gameplay identity.
Historical Total War titles are generally a tighter fit than Warhammer because they preserve more of the same campaign/battle expectations, even when specific mechanics differ.

title: Battle Cry of Freedom

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Battle Cry of Freedom as a large-scale multiplayer PvP combat game centered on historically grounded Civil War battles with first- and third-person gunplay, artillery, cavalry, engineer support, melee bayonet/sabre fighting, realistic projectile physics, destructible battlefields, and commander-led tactical play. The strongest identity is tactical shooter: match-based battlefield engagements, realism-leaning weapon behavior, role-based military units, and emphasis on tactical cunning over narrative or RPG progression. It also overlaps with military_fps due to historical armed combat and firearm focus, but the scale, role variety, and tactical realism make tactical_shooter the better primary fit. Evidence for MMO-like scale exists in store genres and 500-600 player battles, but not enough to justify an MMO RPG archetype.

title: AI War 2

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify AI War 2 as a 'grand strategy/RTS hybrid' centered on galactic-scale territorial expansion, fleet and defense-network management, multiple factions with unique economies and rules, and real-time simulation with pause/speed controls. The core loop is long-form campaign play where the player starts from a tiny foothold, expands carefully, manages fronts, optimizes empire and fleet composition, and eventually strikes key AI leaders. The text repeatedly emphasizes macro decision-making, automation, tactical optimization, and large-scale systemic simulation rather than match-based or action-oriented play, which strongly fits grand_strategy with RTS and 4X overlap.

overall_note: The set is small but credible. Distant Worlds: Universe is a notably strong gameplay neighbor because of its pausable real-time space strategy, automation, and galaxy-scale simulation. Stellaris is also reasonable, but it is more conventional grand strategy/4X and less specifically aligned with AI War 2's RTS-hybrid structure and pressure model.

title: Killer7

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame Killer7 as a single-player, story-driven action-adventure with a deeply authored linear campaign, a dark surreal tone, puzzle-solving, character-switching, and hybrid first/third-person shooting encounters. The evidence clearly supports linear progression, encounter-based ranged combat, environmental/puzzle elements, and a strong authored narrative. It does not cleanly match shooter-specialist archetypes like military_fps, tactical_shooter, or third_person_cover_shooter, and the horror is present more as tone and imagery than as pure survival-horror structure, so cinematic_action_adventure is the best fit with action_horror as a secondary influence.

overall_note: The rail has a couple of strong stylistic and structural neighbors, but it also contains one clear metadata/game mismatch and several candidates that lean more on creator tone or visual surrealism than Killer7's unusual guided-path shooter-puzzle identity.
anchor_summary: Killer7 is a highly authored single-player cult action-adventure: linear stage progression, on-rails/guided movement, encounter-based shooting, puzzle solving, character-switching for abilities, boss gates, and a surreal horror-tinged narrative presentation. Its closest fits should emphasize structured campaign play, stylized shooting/adventure hybridity, and eccentric authored progression over open exploration, loot, or multiplayer systems.
gap_notes: No candidate really matches Killer7's signature guided/rail-like traversal plus first-person targeting and character-switch puzzle structure.

title: The Eternal Cylinder

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently depict a single-player, third-person 3D open-world survival adventure centered on exploring an alien planet, evading a world-destroying cylinder, solving environmental puzzles, and adapting a herd via mutations that unlock traversal and survival abilities. title: Mordheim: City of the Damned - Witch Hunters

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Accepted via DLC inheritance from the parent game because the DLC is clearly additive: it adds a new Witch Hunters warband, unit types, and a playable campaign, with no evidence of a different core mode or genre. Parent-game stored descriptions explicitly identify Mordheim: City of the Damned as a turn-based tactical game where you lead warbands into lethal skirmishes, blending RPG progression, unit customization, gear, mission-based battles, and multiplayer. The DLC text reinforces the same warband-based tactical structure through unit classes, weapons, skills, spellcasting, and campaign content. This strongly supports turn_based_tactics as primary, with tactical_rpg as secondary due to unit growth, customization, and RPG progression layers.

overall_note: The set contains the essential base-game pairing plus several strong tactical-skirmish neighbors centered on squad management, campaign progression, and punishing attrition. Lower-ranked entries drift somewhat in combat format or structure, but all remain defensible genre-adjacent comps.
anchor_summary: DLC warband for Mordheim built on the parent game's turn-based tactical skirmishes: small-unit warband combat, campaign progression, gear/skill customization, and harsh persistent roster consequences.
gap_notes: The base game is mandatory and correctly belongs at the top as a direct parent/DLC pairing.
Battle Brothers is an especially strong non-identical comp because it matches persistent roster management, injuries/losses, and tactical battles better than broader squad tactics games.
XCOM 2 is a strong rail inclusion, though its mission structure and higher-level strategy layer make it a bit less warband-skirmish-specific than Battle Brothers.
Mechanicus fits the turn-based tactics lane, but it is less about persistent injury-riddled warband skirmishing and closer to authored squad missions.

Necromunda: Underhive Wars shares the closest tabletop-skirmish adaptation spirit and persistent gang growth, but its real-time-with-pause style makes it less gameplay-identical than the purely turn-based candidates above it.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Direct parent game for this DLC; same turn-based warband tactics, campaign flow, customization, and persistent injury/permadeath systems.
Very close on small-band turn-based tactics, roster growth, equipment management, and punishing long-term losses, though its overworld sandbox differs from Mordheim's mission structure.
Strong match on turn-based squad combat, soldier builds, and campaign risk management, but it is more cover-shooter/mission-command focused than warband skirmish RPG.
Clear turn-based tactics overlap with squad customization and campaign progression, but it is more authored and ability-centric, with less of Mordheim's persistent gritty warband attrition feel.
Strong shared identity as a tabletop-derived gang skirmish game with customization and campaign advancement, but its real-time tactical execution makes it a looser gameplay match.
